Effect of particle size on the thermal and mechanical properties of
aluminum composites reinforced with SiC and diamond
Zhanqiu Tan a, Zhizhong Chen a,G e n l i a nF a na, Gang Ji b, Jie Zhang a, Run Xu a, Aidang Shan c,
Zhiqiang Li a,,D iZ h a n ga,

b Unit Matriaux et Transformations (UMET), CNRS UMR 8207, Universit Lille 1, 59655 Villeneuve d'Ascq, France

The effect of particle size on thermal conductivity (TC) and bending strength was investigated in the SiC/Al and
diamond/Al (Dia/Al) composites for thermal management applications. The results showed that with the particle
size of SiC increasing from 7 to 250m, the TC of the 40 vol.% SiC/Al composite was improved by 50%, from 182 to
272 W/mK, while the bending strength decreased by 100% from 352 to 175 MPa. Comparatively, with diamond
particles increasing from 30 to 200m, the TC of the 40 vol.% Dia/Al composite increased by around 52% from 313
to 475 W/mK, and the bending strength declined by 32% from 327 to 223 MPa. Characterization of fracture sur-
face indicated that large SiC particles featured by an irregular shape tended to fracture prior to the Al matrix dur-
ing loading, while large diamond particles with a regular shape did not have such a tendency. Moreover, diamond
particles with a high intrinsic TC contributed to global TC much more than SiC ones with a relatively low TC. Thus,
in order to balance thermal and mechanical properties of particulate reinforced aluminum composites, particle
size of reinforcements should be well compromised according to their intrinsic properties.

1. Introduction
With increasing power density and minimization of electronic
components, the need for developing ef cient thermal management
materials with a high thermal conductivity (TC) and a low coef cient
of thermal expansion (CTE) has become urgent [13]. To meet this
requirement, aluminum matrix composites reinforced with SiC and/or
diamond particles (hereafter SiC/Al and Dia/Al composites for short,
respectively) have been developed in the past decades [39]. SiC/Al
composite, with the TC typically in the range of 180 270 W/mK [8,10,
11], is popularly used nowadays due mainly to its low cost and
low CTE being compatible with that of electronic components, while
Dia/Al composite with the much higher TC of around 350 750 W/mK
[57,1215], is the potential candidate for a niche market where specic
products with an extremely high TC are required.
In general, global TCs of both SiC/Al and Dia/Al composites are
determined by intrinsic TCs of SiC (244 400 W/mK for 6H-SiC [5,8,10,
1619]), diamond particles (1200 2000 W/mK [4,6,13,15]) and pure
Al matrix (around 220 237 W/mK [5,13]) as well as the SiC/Al and
Dia/Al interfaces. The resistance of thermal exchange across an interface
can be described by interfacial thermal resistance (ITR). It has been re-
ported that ITR can be signi cantly tailored by processing conditions
[6,20], surface coating of reinforcement particles [12,14,2123] and
matrix alloying [24,25]. Although the effect of chemical nature of inter-
facial bonding and the bonding strength on ITR remains unclear[10,15,
26], the common understanding is that at the given volume fraction of
particles, the use of ne particles introduces large interface area,
which increases the total thermal resistance and, in turn results in a
low global TC.

low global TC. For example, it has been reported that the TC of the
liquid-inltrated 65 vol.% Dia/Al composite increased from 400 to
760 W/mK by increasing diamond particle size from 30 to 150 m
[27]. In addition, efforts have been devoted to increasing the volume
fraction of particles to enhance the global TC. For example, in the
liquid-inltrated SiC/Al composites, the content of SiC particles was as
high as 74 vol.% via a bimodal particle size distribution, which increased
the TC from 193 to 228 W/mK[9]. Hence, both increases of size and vol-
ume fraction of reinforcement particles are two practical means to im-
prove global TC of these composites.
In addition to thermal properties, mechanical properties of such
composites, e.g. bending strength, is also rigidly required for thermal
management applications according to their functions of mechanically
supporting protection and heat dissipation for electronic components
[2830]. As previously reported [31], the strengthening mechanisms
of particulate reinforced metal matrix composites (PRMMCs) are two-
fold. Firstly, load can be effectively transferred from the plastic matrix
to the hard and rigid particles, while large particles are prone to fracture
themselves during deformation, thus the pre-existing cracks initiate

fractures and cannot ensure load transfer. For example, the yield
strength and plastic work hardening rate of the SiC/Al composites
would decrease with increasing the particle size [31]. Secondly, the
metal matrix surrounding the particles can be strengthened by increas-
ing dislocation density due to thermal mismatch during cooling. But the
effect of thermal mismatch strengthening would be weakened with re-
duced interface area by using large particles. It should be noted that the
content of rigid particles in most of PRMMCs for structural applications
is usually below 40 vol.%, since a higher content seriously degrades the
deforming ability. Contrary to thermal properties of the PRMMCs, it's
not benecial for mechanical properties by using large particles with a
high content. Hence, a compromised solution should be proposed to de-
sign the SiC/Al and Dia/Al composites for thermal management applica-
tions in order to have high TC values without scarifying necessary
bending strength.
In our previous work, the SiC/Al and Dia/Al composites, with TCs as
high as around 270 and 570 W/mK [10,13] respectively, have been pro-
duced by vacuum hot pressing (VHP) under the optimized conditions.
This work aims to further study the effect of particle size variation on
the TC and bending strength of SiC/Al and Dia/Al composites. It also pro-
vides an example demonstrating that, the signi cance of simply using
large reinforcement particles should be conscientiously reconsidered
in the design of PRMMCs for thermal management applications.

in purity were used as the matrix material. Green  -SiC particles with

thetic diamond particles (type HWD40) of 30, 75 and 200 m, were
used as the reinforcements. As shown in Fig. 1 , typically the SiC and

3.2. Effect of particle size on bending strength and fractography
Bending strength of the SiC/Al and Dia/Al composites was shown in
Fig. 3. For both composites, the bending strength decreased monotoni-
cally with increasing particle size. For the SiC/Al composites, the highest
bending strength of around 350 MPa was obtained with the particle size
of 7 m, while the lowest one was around 175 MPa with the particle size
of 250 m, indicating a decline of 50%. For the Dia/Al composites, the
highest bending strength of 327 MPa was obtained when the particle
size was 30 m, being close to the highest one in the SiC/Al composites.
With the particle size increasing to 200m, the bending strength of the
Dia/Al composites decreased by around 32% to 223 MPa.

4.2. Effect of particle size on bending strength and fractography
According toFig. 3, for the SiC/Al composites, a sharp decrease in the
bending strength (by around 50%) occurs when the particle size
increases from 40 to 160 m, while no such a feature can be observed
in the Dia/Al composites. This difference could be explained by the dim-
ples and SiC (or Dia) particles observed in the fracture surface ( Figs. 4
and 5).
Dimples are the direct consequences of plastic deformation of the Al
matrix. The presence of fractures in the SiC particles themselves indi-
cates that the large particles tend to fracture prior to the Al matrix.
This can lead to crack propagation and decrease load bearing efciency
from the metal matrix, as evidenced by the decreased number of dim-
ples with the particle size increasing.
According to Fig. 5 , in the Dia/Al composites reinforced with the
particles of 30 and 200 m in size, diamond particles were tightly
embedded in Al matrix and no interfacial fracture but dimples and
residual Al could be seen, which indicates a strong interfacial bond-
ing and suf cient load transfer. As shown in Figs.

embedded in Al matrix and no interfacial fracture but dimples and
residual Al could be seen, which indicates a strong interfacial bond-
ing and suf cient load transfer. As shown in Figs. 4 and 5 , the particle
fracture behaviors are probably mainly governed by their shape and
intrinsic strength. The predominant causes for the decline of bending
strength are different in the SiC/Al and Dia/Al composites. Due to the
fracture of large SiC particles themselves, load transfer and matrix
deformation are both seriously diminished in the SiC/Al composites
reinforced with large particles, thus the bending strength drastically
declines. However, the dimples in the matrix instead of particle frac-
tures are clearly visible in the Dia/Al composites reinforced with
large particles, thus their declined bending strength mainly results
from the reduced interface area, which in turn weakens the strength-
ening of interfacial load transfer and thermal mismatch.
4.3. Effect of particle size on the comprehensive performances
To consider the comprehensive performance of both composites, the
disadvantages of large SiC particles appeared to be obvious. As shown in
Fig. 3a, the use of large SiC particles, e.g. with particle size larger than
70 m, will lead to a major degradation in bending strength, but have
a very limited effect on TC. The nonlinear changes in mechanical and
thermal properties make the SiC particle size of 40 m being the most
suitable for comprehensive performance. In contrast, the use of large di-
amond particles will contribute to a linear TC enhancement, even with
particle size larger than 200 m, while maintain a moderate linear de-
creased bending strength.

It can be inferred that, with particle sizes increasing, the TC and
bending strength of PRMMCs strongly depends on intrinsic characteris-
tics of the incorporated particles. The higher intrinsic TC of the particles
is, a higher critical particle size is expected, and a global TC increase can
be achieved in a broader range of particle size. The bending strength of
particulate reinforced composites decreases more drastically when the
particles have lower intrinsic strength or irregular shape, a transition
in failure mechanism, from the plastic fracture of matrix to the brittle
fracture of particles, may even lead to a nonlinear decline. Therefore,
particle size should be optimized to neither exceed the critical value
inducing a marginal increment of Kr
eff, nor lead to large-scale transition
in failure mechanism. As a result, the permitted upper limit of particle
size is higher for those particles with regular shape and high strength,
e.g. diamond, than those with irregular shape and lower strength, e.g.
SiC. Thus, the real value by using large particles to improve thermal
property, irrespective of the mechanical property of the composites,
should be discreetly considered in the design of PRMMCs for thermal
management applications.
5. Conclusions
The effect of particle size on TC and bending strength of the VHPed
SiC/Al and Dia/Al composites was studied. The main conclusions can
be drawn as follows:
1) Large SiC particles with a low intrinsic TC contributed marginally to
the global TC of the SiC/Al composites, but seriously degraded the
bending strength. With SiC particle size increasing from 7 to
250 m, the TC of the SiC/Al composites increased from 182 to
272 W/mK, while the bending strength decreased from 353 to
170 MPa.
2) Large diamond particles with a high intrinsic TC greatly contributed
to the global TC of the Dia/Al composites, and slightly degrade their
bending strength. With diamond particle size increasing from 30 to
250 m, the TC of the Dia/Al composites linearly increased fro

d
to the global TC of the Dia/Al composites, and slightly degrade their
bending strength. With diamond particle size increasing from 30 to
250 m, the TC of the Dia/Al composites linearly increased from
313 to 475 W/mK, while the bending strength linearly decreased
from 327 to 223 MPa.
3) The different responses of SiC and diamond particles to the thermal
and mechanical properties of the composites are mainly attributed
to their different intrinsic properties. Large SiC particles tend to frac-
ture during deformation and thus deteriorate the bending strength
of the composites, while large diamond particles do not. This study
indicates that, in the design of thermal management materials, the
signicance of simply using large particles should be conscientiously
considered.

reactions in SiC nanoparticulate reinforced aluminum matrix composites

abstract
The SiC nanoparticulate reinforced Al3.0 wt.% Mg composites were fabricated by combining pressureless
inltration with ball-milling and cold-pressing technology at 700 C for 2 h. The effects of SiC nanopar-
ticulate volume fractions (6%, 10% and 14%) and Al particulate sizes (38 /H9262m and 74 /H9262m) on interfacial
reactions were investigated by SEM, TEM and X-ray diffraction. The results show that the MgO at the
interface between SiC nanoparticulate and molten Al can provide a barrier for the diffusion of Si, C and Al.
Using Al particulate (74 /H9262m) as raw material, the Al
4C3 phase was not found in the composites containing
6 vol.% and 10 vol.% SiC, but presented in the composites containing 14 vol.% SiC. When SiC content up to
14 vol.%, the products of MgO around SiC nanoparticulate are not enough to provide effective protection
from the reaction between SiC and molten Al, therefore the diffusion of Si, C and Al can take place to
produce Al
4 C3 and Si phases. Using 38 /H9262m Al particulate as raw material, the ne Al particulate possesses
the high reaction activity and can easily be embedded into the g

nd Al can take place to
produce Al
4 C3 and Si phases. Using 38 /H9262m Al particulate as raw material, the ne Al particulate possesses
the high reaction activity and can easily be embedded into the gap among the big Mg particulate segre-
gated at the interface, resulting in the appearance of exposure surface of SiCp to the Al and the forming of
diffusion channels for the atomics C, Si and Al. So, the formations of Al
4 C3 and Si phases were occurred.

1. Introduction
SiC particulate reinforced Al-based metal matrix composites
(MMCs) have been considered as excellent candidates to be applied
as structural material in the aerospace and automobile indus-
try [1]. Compared with the unreinforced matrix alloy, Al-based
MMCs have attractive physical and mechanical properties, such
as high strength, high hardness and superior wear resistance. It
has been reported that higher mechanical strength of particulate
reinforced metal matrix composites can be achieved by decreas-
ing the reinforcement particulate size [25] . Nanoparticulate as
the reinforcement can signicantly increase the matrix mechan-
ical strength by more effectively promoting particle hardening
mechanisms than micron size particulate [6]. However, the possi-
ble chemical interfacial reactions between the ne SiC particulate
(especially nanoparticulate) and Al matrix play a crucial role in
determining the mechanical property of Al matrix composites. The
very high ratio of surface to volume of SiC nanoparticulate induces
the higher interfacial reaction activity than micron size partic-
ulates. In order to obtain excellent mechanical properties, it is
indispensable to form desired interfaces.

Some interfacial reactions in metal matrix composites can
effectively improve the mechanical properties of composites by
enhancing of the wettability between particulates and molten
metal [710] , but some of interfacial reactions also cause the degra-
dation of mechanical properties due to the formation of brittle and
unstable phases (e.g., Al
4C3). The reaction between molten Al and
SiC particulates in the temperature range from 675 C to 900 C has
been widely investigated, producing Al4C3 and silicon according to
the following reaction equation [1114] :
4Al + 3SiC  Al4C3 + 3Si (1)
However, the existing investigations on the interfacial reactions
in metal matrix composites are mainly related to three topics for
controlling the interfacial reactions: (1) control of matrix compo-
sition, such as SiC/Al composites. The above interfacial reactions
can be suppressed to hinder the formation of Al
4C3 phase by
adding Si element into Al matrix alloy [1517] . (2) Selection of pro-
cess parameters, for example, fabrication temperature and holding
time, etc. [18,19]. (3) Surface modication of reinforcement, for
instance, the coating or oxidation of SiC particulate [20]. Thus, little
attention has been paid to the effects of SiC nanoparticulate vol-
ume fraction and aluminum matrix particulate size on interfacial
reactions in SiC nanoparticulate reinforced aluminum matrix com-
posites. And from the available literatures, the investigations about
these effects on interfacial reactions are limited to providing the

2. Experimental procedure
The Al (the mean size approximate to 38/H9262m and 74 /H9262m) with purity of 99.5 wt.%
and 3 wt.% Mg (approximately 74 /H9262m) with purity of 99.6 wt.% were used as matrix
and reinforced with 6 vol.%, 10 vol.% and 14 vol.% SiC (40 nm) respectively. The
composites designations are summarized in Table 1 . High-energy ball milling has
been widely utilized for mixing SiC nanoparticulate and Al particulate. High-energy
ball milling was performed in a planetary ball milling using 200 hardened stain-
less steel balls of 4 g each. The ratio of ball to particulate weight was 4:1. The
rotational speed was controlled at 450 rpm. These particulates were dry mixed
for 10 h in a high purity of 99.99% argon atmosphere to avoid element oxidation.
The mixed particulates were compressed to prepare the preform with dimensions
of 40 mm  40 mm 80 mm by the cold pressing technology under a pressure of
50 GPa.
The identical composition with matrix material was melted in a graphite cru-
cible kept in an electric resistance furnace, using a ux mixture of salts (KCl + NaCl) to
decrease oxidation of material by excluding oxygen and provide a protective atmo-
sphere inside the electric resistance furnace. When the temperature of furnace was
raised to 700
C and held for 20 min, the preforms for pressureless inltration were
put into and suspended inside the molten material fo

electric resistance furnace. When the temperature of furnace was
raised to 700
C and held for 20 min, the preforms for pressureless inltration were
put into and suspended inside the molten material for 2 h, and subsequently the
composites were cooled inside furnace.
The microstructural characterization specimens were prepared by grinding
paper from 320 to 1200 grit and metallographically polished with 1 /H9262m alumina,
and subsequently ultrasonically cleaned and etched using a reagent comprising 5 ml
HF and 95 ml distilled water. The microstructures were examined using a MeF-3
optical microscope and a JSMT-200 scanning electron microscope. D/MAX-IIIB X-
ray diffraction was performed on the bulk specimens for identication of phases.
The interface between the Al matrix and SiC nanoparticulate was investigated using
JEM200 transmission electron microscope.

4. Conclusions
The SiC nanoparticulate reinforced Al matrix composites was
fabricated by combining pressureless inltration with ball-milling
and cold-pressing technology at 700
C for 2 h. The effects of SiC vol-
ume fraction and Al matrix particulate size on interfacial reactions
between SiC nanoparticulate and the molten Al were investigated.
The results obtained are summarized as follows:
When Mg was added to the Al matrix, the formations of MgO
at the interface between SiC nanoparticulate and molten Al were
found, which can enhance the wettability and provide a barrier for
the diffusion of Si, C and Al.
Using Al particulate (74 /H9262m) as raw material, the Al
4C3 phase
was not found in the composites containing SiC nanoparticulate
6 vol.% and 10 vol.% respectively, but present in the composites
containing SiC nanoparticulate 14 vol.%. When SiC content up to
14 vol.%, the products of MgO around SiC nanoparticulate are not
enough to form effective protection from the reaction between SiCp
and molten Al, therefore the diffusion of Si, C and Al can take place
and produce Al
4C3 and Si phases.
Using 38 /H9262m Al particulate as raw material, the ner Al particu-
late possesses the high reaction activity and can easily be embedded
into the gap among the big Mg particulate, which segregate at the
interface between SiCp and Al, resulting in the appearance of expo-
sure surface of SiCp to the Al and diffusion channels for the atomics
C, Si and Al.

Investigating the effect of SiC particles on the physical and thermal
properties of Al6061/SiC p composite
Reza Zare a, Hassan Shari  a, *, Mohammad Reza Saeri a, Morteza Tayebi b

abstract
In the present study, the effect of volume fraction of SiC reinforcement particles on the physical and
thermal properties of Al matrix composite (6061) was investigated. In this regard, composite powders
with different reinforcement contents were prepared using hot press molding. It was seen that with
increasing the amount of the reinforcement, the density of the samples decreased, while the porosity
increased. Microhardness tests indicated that there was a direct relationship between the hardness and
the amount of the reinforcement. SEM images con rmed the proper distribution of the reinforcing
particles in the matrix. Dilatometry tests exhibited the decline of coef cient of thermal expansion by
improving the amount of reinforcement. XFA test was also used to determine the thermal conductivity of
the samples and it was seen that with increasing SiC content in the composite samples, the thermal
conductivity declined to 61.5 W/m.K due to enhancement of crystalline defects in the phase boundaries.
Finally, the experimental results were compared to thermoelastic models.

1. Introduction
Al/SiC composites are of the most well-known composites for
their high thermal conductivity, low thermal expansion coef cient
along with good wear resistance that play an important role in
modern technology [ 1e3].

thermal properties can be obtained [4]. Although Al/SiC composites
are currently the subject of many investigations, less attention has
been attended to their physical and thermal properties, and in
particular their dimensional stability. In electronic industries, it is
vital to simultaneously reduce the size of circuit components and
improve the power density. Thus, there is always an ever-increasing
need for new high strength materials with high thermal conduc-
tivity and low thermal expansion coef cient to maximize the heat
dissipation and minimize the thermal shock, respectively [ 1,3]. In
the following different features of aluminum matrix composites for
thermal applications are discussed:
1.1. Thermal conductivity of the composites
There are several mechanisms for explaining the thermal con-
ductivity or, in other words, the transfer of thermal energy within a
solid object. Various carriers, such as electrons, phonons (atomic
vibrations) or photons, are considered for the transfer of thermal
energy in a solid. In non-metallic materials, phonon transfer is
considered as the main mechanism for heat transfer, while in
metals heat is almost entirely transferred with the assistance of
electrons. In alloys and semiconductors, both mechanisms are
responsible for transmission of thermal energy. In general, thermal
conductivity depends on the distribution of phonons [ 5,6]. In fact,
thermal resistance is due to various types of processes, such as
phonon-phonon scattering, scattering within boundaries, scat-
tering by structural defects, etc. In order to maximize the thermal
conductivity, phonon scattering processes must reach to their
minimum values [ 7].

conductivity, phonon scattering processes must reach to their
minimum values [ 7]. Increasing the thermal conductivity of the
composites depends on a large number of variables that causes
different properties of the material.

1.3. Reinforcement surface modi cation
Investigation into the effect of reinforcement surface modi ca-
tion was carried out to provide better interactions between matrix
and reinforcement, and evaluate its effect on the thermal conduc-
tion coef cient. Generally, surface modi cation of the reinforce-
ment improves the reinforcement stability in the matrix, since
surface modi cation prevents particle agglomeration it could in-
crease the mixability of the matrix and reinforcement only if the
proper modier is selected [ 9,10].
1.4. Suitable reinforcement orientation
The proper orientation of the reinforcement particles has a
signicant effect on the thermal conductivity and nal properties of
the composite. This is particularly important for non-spherical re-
inforcements such as carbon nanotube, graphite/graphene and
two-dimensional reinforcements such as boron nitride. The dis-
advantages of the thermal conduction behavior of the one-
dimensional reinforcements are not discussed for two-
dimensional reinforcements which is due to the high thermal
conductivity of the two-dimensional materials and high surface-to-
volume ratio of planar particles. The important point is the proper
orientation of these plains, because of the anisotropy of planar
reinforcements, i.e. when they are arranged perpendicular to the C-
axis, their thermal conductivity are several times higher than those
which are arranged along the C-axis. Therefore, preparation of
composites with a speci c orientation reinforcement, causes the
composite thermal conductivity to change very signi cantly espe-
cially in the case of planar reinforcements that are arranged hori-
zontally or vertically in the matrix [ 9,11].
1.5. The effect of matrix/reinforcement interface
One of the factors affecting thermal conductivity of the com-
posites is the type of matrix/reinforcement interface. In this regard,
formation of new compounds affects the heat transfer mechanism.

1.6. The effect of manufacturing process
The composite manufacturing process affects the thermal con-
ductivity. As a result, oxidation possibility, introduction of external
contaminations, nal porosity and density, and the formation of
unwanted compounds, which can differ nal heat transfer rate of
the composite, are all depend on the preparation method. The re-
searchers observed that by producing Al/diamond composites via
SQ and GPI methods, the GPI method, due to protection of melt
against the oxidizing agents by inert gas and the gas pressure,
provide much less porosities and the samples with higher density.
Thus, the thermal conduction of the composite increased signi -
cantly [12,13].

2. Experimental procedure
2.1. Materials
In order to prepare the composite samples, SiC (45 mm, 99.8%,
Merck) and 6061 aluminum alloy (45 mm, 99.9%, Merck) powders
were used. The chemical composition and morphology of the
powder particles are brought in Table 1 and Fig. 1, respectively. For
uniform mixing of the powders, mechanical milling process was
employed by a high-energy ball mill. Powder mixtures with
different reinforcement contents (5, 10, 15 and 20 vol%) were milled
at 600 rpm for 2 h. For all experiments, stainless steel cup con-
taining 5 stainless steel balls with average diameter of 20 mm and
ball-to-powder weight ratio of 10:1 were utilized [27]. 1 wt% stearic
acid was used as process controlling agent to prevent the powder
particles from agglomeration. Argon gas (99.99% pure) was used as
milling atmosphere.

ratio of 10:1 were utilized [27]. 1 wt% stearic
acid was used as process controlling agent to prevent the powder
particles from agglomeration. Argon gas (99.99% pure) was used as
milling atmosphere. The powder mixture was hot-pressed in a
cylindrical die with 140 mm length and 7 mm inner diameter at
450 /C14 C for 30 min under 500 MPa pressure. Additionally, 2 graphite
disks with 5 mm thickness were placed on the both ends of the
sample.
2.2. Microhardness and density
Microhardness of the sintered samples was measured by Leitz
microhardness under 500 g load for 15 s. In order to increase the
accuracy, microhardness tests were repeated on four different spots
and the average value was reported. To determine the density and
porosity percent of the samples, Archimedes method was used
according to ASTM B31 1-08 standard.
2.3. Characterization
In order to characterize the microstructure, particle distribution,
size and morphology of the powder particles scanning electron
microscopy (SEM) (LEO model 435VP, Netherlands) was used. In
this way, samples were coated with gold to increase their
conductivity.
2.4. Thermal examinations
2.4.1. Dilatometry test
Dilatometry test was done by (BEHER model DIL801, Germany)
on samples with 6 mm diameter and 14 mm length. Coef cient of
thermal expansion and dimensional changes of the samples were
measured in the temperature range of 25 e450 /C14 C with heating rate
of 5 /C14 C/min. This process was done for 3 heating and cooling cycles.
The length tolerance of the diameter was considered 0.1 mm.
2.4.2. Xenon ash thermal diffusivity analysis
Thermal diffusivity and thermal conductivity coef cient (K)
were obtained using xenon ash thermal diffusivity analyzer (XFA
model 500, Germany) based on ASTM E1461 standard. Samples
with 12.7 mm diameter and 5 mm thickness were exposed to high-
intensity energy pulse. The energy of the pulse is absorbed by the
samples, thus thermal diffusivity of the sample is calculated based
on the thickness and the time required for temperature elevation of
the

samples, thus thermal diffusivity of the sample is calculated based

Fig. 2 shows the SEM micrographs of samples containing 5, 10,

from the mixing operation. Fig. 3 shows a magni cation of the

powder particles are well bonded to each other, no crack is
observed at the matrix/reinforcement interface, and it was found
that press force, temperature, and sintering time were appropriate
during the manufacturing process.
3.2. Hardness measurement
Fig. 4 shows that the hardness of the composite samples
improved with increasing SiC volume fraction. This can be attrib-
uted to the presence of hard silicon carbide particles in the soft
aluminum matrix, which increases the density of crystalline de-
fects, especially dislocations. Furthermore, silicon carbide particles
act as obstacles to the motion of dislocations [ 19]. According to the
graph, the hardening rate from declined with increasing the
amount of reinforcement when the reinforcement content
increased from 15 vol% to 20 vol%. It can be said that by increasing
the amount of silicon carbide, compression of the powder becomes
more dif cult, resulting in porosity between the particles, which

3.3. Density of the prepared specimens after sintering
Fig. 5 shows the relative density changes and the porosity of the
samples in terms of SiC content. As can be seen, the samples density
reduced with increasing SiC content. Since SiC is a hard material,
the compressibility declined by rising the SiC percentage. Due to
the large difference between the melting points of the matrix and
the reinforcement, with enhancing the SiC content, the sintering
process of the powder was disrupted, resulting in formation of
porosities and cracks in the composite. Other reason contributing
to the reduction of density is agglomeration, which increases with
improving the SiC content. As a result, the formed small porosities
inside agglomerates and large porosities between them increased
the porosity and reduced the density [ 20]. Removing these
porosities requires applying high pressures and elevating the
heating time. This enhancement of temperature causes the grain
growth and loss of mechanical properties [ 21]. Low SiC density
compared to aluminum is also another factor in reducing the
density of the composite sample, i.e. by increasing the amount of
reinforcement, density of the samples reduced. By comparing the
apparent and the theoretical densities in Fig. 6, it is apparent that at
rst the difference of the two values was low, but by enhancing the
amount of reinforcement the difference increased. This is because
the effect of porosity is not considered in calculating the theoretical
density. Due to the lack of powder compaction, samples containing
large amounts of reinforcement had higher porosities in the way

Fig. 14shows the variations of CTE in terms of the porosity of the

samples increased with increasing the reinforcement content. It
affects the heat transfer rate of the composite, so the existing
porosity reduces the thermal conductivity of the composite, and in
fact acts as an interface by creating thermal resistance. One of the
possible reasons for this phenomenon is the tendency of porosities
to accumulate at phase boundaries. By increasing the density of
porosities at the interface areas, thermal conductivity decreases
considerably. This phenomenon shows the reason for the difference
between theoretical and empirical results.
Fig. 15 shows the variation of thermal conduction coef cient of

the direct relation of these parameters with each other in a way
that by increasing the thermal expansion coef cient, heat con-
duction of the samples increased. The lower the amount of rein-
forcement in the composite, the lower the density of dislocations at
the interfaces, which results in a higher atomic order in these areas.
Therefore, it can be concluded that with increasing the SiC content
in Al/SiC composite samples, the density of crystalline defects such
as dislocations in the boundary regions increased and reduced the
atomic order. Thus, according to Rayleigh's model for thermal
conductivity analysis of composites; which considers the high heat
conduction as a result of high atomic order, by increasing the
amount of reinforcement the density of dislocations in the
boundary regions increased. Subsequently, the thermal expansion
and thermal conductivity values reduced.

4. Conclusions
(1) The sample preparation conditions were selected as 30 min
sintering time and 500 MPa pressure. Hardness improved by
increasing SiC content. In a way that by adding 20 vol% SiC,
the microhardness was reached to 127 HV. Due to the
disruption of the powder compaction process and agglom-
eration, by enhancing the amount of SiC in the composite
samples porosity increased and density declined.
(2) The thermal expansion coef cient of the samples decreased
linearly by elevating the SiC content, so that the CTE of
sample containing 20 vol% reinforcement declined to
14.8 ppm/K.
(3) The suggested thermostatic models were unable to analyze
the CTE diagrams accurately, since they do not consider the
created internal strains.
(4) The prepared samples by the mentioned method had proper
thermal stability in heating and cooling cycles.
(5) At temperatures close to 400
/C14 C, due to the release of ther-
moelastic stress and the sudden change of SiC thermal
behavior, the CTE values of the samples improved
signicantly.
(6) With increasing SiC content in the composite samples, the
thermal conductivity decreased.
(7) By increasing the SiC content, crystalline defects enhanced in
phase boundaries. For this reason the thermal conductivity of
the sample containing 20 vol% declined to 61.5 W/m.K.

AbstractIn this study, based on the fracture surface observations using an SEM (scanning electron micro-
scope), a numerical unit model and 3-dimensional non-uniform model for FEM (nite element method)
analysis are proposed by using Gursons model as a constitutive equation. The e/C128ect of the non-uniform
distribution of the SiC particle volume fraction and aspect ratio in the matrix are considered for the evalu-
ation of the stressstrain relationship and the damage. The results show that the dimple fracture process of
matrix aluminum alloy is well simulated, after a large amount of plastic deformation. The non-uniformity
of the SiC particle volume fraction, aspect ratio and its locations have a strong e/C128ect on the local and glo-
bal damage behavior and stressstrain relationships. It is shown that the fracture strain increases largely
when the aspect ratio of SiC particles is 1.0 and it is distributed uniformly in the matrix. The numerical
model is improved by considering the non-uniform e/C128ect on the tensile stress, fracture strain and local and
global fracture. # 1998 Acta Metallurgica Inc.

site material is decreased by increasing the volume

However, the use of this material is limited by its
low fracture toughness and ductility. In this ma-

The experimental analyses have been conducted
by Kamat et al . [11], Flom et al . [12], Tao et al . [6]
and Kikuchi et al . [13]. These authors showed that
the damage of this material is due to the dimple
fracture of the matrix, particle cracking and the
debonding between a particle and the matrix.
Kamat et al . indicated that the yield strength
increases with decreasing particle spacing. Flom et
al. concluded that the fracture behavior of this ma-
terial mainly depends on the particle volume frac-
tion. Tao et al . showed that the fracture toughness
of this material decreases with the increase of the
standard deviation of local particle volume fraction
and nearest particle spacing. Kikuchi et al. showed
that the volume fraction of SiC particles on the
fracture surface is much larger than the average
value. Apparently the fracture occurs where the SiC
particle volume fraction is comparatively larger
than in other areas and the nal fracture occurs as
a result of the linkage of these locally fractured
areas.
The numerical damage analyses of SiC whisker
or SiC particles reinforced aluminum alloys have
been conducted by Christman et al . [14], Kikuchi et
al. [13, 15], Finot et al . [16] and Shen et al . [17]

determine an exact measurement of interface mech-

their aspect ratios are also considered in the global
damage process.

5. DISCUSSION
The present work has qualitatively identied the
uniform and non-uniform damage process, as duc-
tile failure in the local and global regions and
obtained some reasonable damage phenomena
through experimentation. However, the stressstrain
relationships of each unit used in the non-uniform
models are tted from a uni-axial tension condition
(uniform stress triaxiality condition as 0.33) and the
unit models do not consider the e/C128ect of residual
stress, particle mutual interaction, interface debond-
ing and particle cracking.
The thermal residual stresses have been studied
by Shen et al . [17].

odels do not consider the e/C128ect of residual
stress, particle mutual interaction, interface debond-
ing and particle cracking.
The thermal residual stresses have been studied
by Shen et al . [17]. The results show that the ther-
mal residual stresses have a strong e/C128ect on the
overall constitutive response during subsequent
loading in tension or compression. Due to Shen et
al. results, the e/C128ect of residual stress can be con-
sidered by changing the stressstrain relationships
of the matrix slightly. If so the main results of the
paper on the fracture process of the models do not
change qualitatively. Moreover, it is extremely di-
cult to obtain the local residual stress eld exper-
imentally. For these reasons the analysis of the
thermal residual stress is not considered in this
study.

pattern as local dimple fracture, interface debond-
ing and particle cracking may vary under locally
di/C128erent constraint or stress triaxiality conditions.
The non-uniform stress triaxiality or constraint
e/C128ects may then a/C128ect the local and global damage
process, but this problem is not considered in this
work. The e/C128ects of stress triaxiality and constraint
have been discussed by many authors [30, 31] using
unit cell models by changing the stress triaxiality,
with and without side constraint conditions. The
results show that the microscopic fracture process is
well simulated using a unit cell model qualitatively.
However, the quantitative analysis of stressstrain
relationships also have some diculties. The reason
for this is that the stress triaxiality and the side con-
straint change largely not only with the changes of
the distance, location and geometrical shape of the
particles but also during the deformation history.

6. CONCLUDING REMARKS
The e/C128ect of the non-uniformity of the SiC par-
ticle reinforced aluminum alloy on the dimple frac-
ture process is studied using FEM analysis. The
e/C128ect of non-uniform distribution of the particle
and particle aspect ratio are evaluated using a non-
uniform model. The following results are obtained.
(1) The SiC particle volume fraction and aspect
ratio largely a/C128ect the stressstrain relationships
and their e/C128ects increase by increasing the particle
volume fraction. When the SiC particle is uniformly
distributed in the base matrix, the tensile strength
increases by increasing the particle aspect ratio. The
fracture strain increases with the increase of the Ap
value up to Ap = 1.0 and it decreases again for
Ap>1.0.
(2) Through the analysis of a non-uniform
model, it is shown that the local fracture occurs
where the local particle volume fraction is the lar-
gest and/or the Ap value in local areas the maxi-
mum or minimum. The global fracture occurs by
linkage of these locally fractured areas.
(3) According to the results of (1) and (2), it is
pointed out that the mechanical properties of this
composite material may be improved if the particle
aspect ratios are close to 1.0 and the particles are
distributed uniformly in the base matrix. In order
to increase the ultimate strength and fracture strain
of the material, it may be better to increase the par-
ticle volume fraction rather than increasing the par-
ticle aspect ratios if the SiC particle is rigid and
perfectly bound to the matrix.

Particle size is one of the most important factors in 
determining wear properties of particulate-rein- 
forced metal composites. However, it appears to be 
difficult to draw a fundamental conclusion from the 
reports about this problem. Some reports have 
suggested that wear resistance of the composites 
increased with increasing particle size [1, 2], while 
others indicated that an increase in particle size had 
a negligible influence on the wear rate [3]. A further 
problem is that nearly all the studies have been 
carried out with such methods as pin-on-disc or sand 
rubber wheel abrasion tests, in which the sliding 
speed was maintained in a narrow range and the 
applied load in a steady state. In fact, among those 
potential applications of the composites, many cases 
are characterized by both sliding and impact wear 
with sliding speed varying over a large range [4]. It is 
thus necessary to study the effect of particle size on 
wear properties of the composites under a variety of 
experimental conditions. In this work, the effect of 
particle size on wear behaviour of SiC particulate- 
reinforced 2024A1 composites has been investigated 
using three tests: sliding wear, impact abrasion and 
erosion. 
Composites containing 15 vol % SiC particles of 
3.5, 10 and 20/~m in size were prepared by the P/M 
process and machined into specimens 30 x 
25  3 mm. The sliding wear test (SWT) was per- 
formed on a reciprocating grooving tester. The slider 
was made of Si3N4 ceramic with the top plane 
2 x 2 mm. The sliding speed was 0.01 m s -1 and the 
normal load about 2.2 MPa. The impact abrasion 
test (IAT) was conducted on a single pendulum 
scratch tester.

Effect of Particle Size on the Microstructures
and Mechanical Properties of SiC-Reinforced
Pure Aluminum Composites
Chao Sun, Min Song, Zhangwei Wang, and Yuehui He
(Submitted September 16, 2010; in revised form November 11, 2010)
This article examined the effects of particle size and extrusion on the microstructures and mechanical
properties of SiC particle-reinforced pure aluminum composites produced by powder metallurgy method.
It has been shown that both particle size and extrusion have important effects on the microstructures and
mechanical properties of the composites. The SiC particles distribute more uniformly when the ratio of the
matrix powder size and SiC particle size approaches unity, and the smaller-sized SiC particles tend to
cluster easily. The voids are found to coexist with the clustered and large-sized SiC particles, and they
signicantly decrease the density and mechanical properties of the composites. Extrusion can redistribute
the SiC particles in the matrix and decrease the number of pores, thus make the SiC particles distribute
more uniformly in the matrix, and enhance the interfacial bonding strength. The decrease in the SiC
particle size improves the tensile strength and yield strength, but decreases the ductility of the composites.
Keywords mechanical properties, metal matrix composites,
microstructures, particle size
1. Introduction
Metal matrix composites reinforced with ceramic particles
are of particular interest for a variety of industrial applications
due to their high strength, elastic modulus, fatigue resistance
and wear resistance, as compared with the corresponding
matrix materials (Ref 1-4). Among the different shaped
reinforcements, the composites reinforced with particulate-
shaped reinforcements offer relatively isotropic properties
compared to short ber- or whisker-reinforced counterparts,
and they can also be produced using conventional metal
manufacturing process with low cost (Ref 5, 6).
During the last two decades, a large number of the
investigations have been carried

counterparts,
and they can also be produced using conventional metal
manufacturing process with low cost (Ref 5, 6).
During the last two decades, a large number of the
investigations have been carried out to reveal the strengthening
mechanisms of metal matrix composites (Ref 7-10). For SiC
particle-reinforced aluminum alloy composites, the SiC is the
main strengthening factor. One strengthening mechanism is the
direct strengthening mechanism resulting from the soft Al
matrix transferring the externally applied load to the hard SiC
reinforcements. The second strengthening mechanism is the
indirect strengthening mechanism resulting from the increase in
dislocation density of the Al matrix due to the difference in the
coefcient of thermal expansion between the SiC and the Al
matrix when subjected to cooling from an elevated processing
temperature or heat-treatment temperature. The formation of
dislocations can signicantly increase the strength of the
matrix. It should be noted that the geometrically necessary
dislocations generated during initial matrix plastic deformation
will also strengthen the composites. When a SiC-reinforced Al
composite is loaded, the SiC particles deform less than the
matrix. To avoid initiation of the voids, dislocations are
generated and stored in the matrix to allow for compatible
deformation of the particles and the matrix. These dislocations
are called geometrically necessary dislocations (Ref 11).
It is widely accepted that both the particle distribution and
particle size have important effects on the mechanical proper-
ties of the composites. The voids coexisted with the clustered
SiC particles, and the large-sized SiC particles can be treated as
pre-existing cracks (Ref 12, 13). The particles along with the
voids cannot transfer any load from the soft matrix to the hard
reinforcements, resulting in degraded mechanical properties.
For a composite with a constant particle volume fraction, there
is a close relationship between the particle size and the
deformation behavior of the compos

esulting in degraded mechanical properties.
For a composite with a constant particle volume fraction, there
is a close relationship between the particle size and the
deformation behavior of the composite. The yield strength and
plastic work hardening rate of the composites increase with
decreasing the particle size. Large particles are more prone to
fracture during extrusion process and tensile testing (Ref 14,
15), and cannot transfer any load from the matrix to the
reinforcements.
In this article, the effects of particle size and particle
distribution on the microstructures and mechanical properties of
SiC-reinforced pure Al composites have been studied before
and after extrusion. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and
mechanical testing were used to characterize the microstruc-
tures and mechanical properties of the composites.

purity (weight percentage) was used to prepare the aluminum
powders by powder metallurgy method. The average size of the

3.1 Microstructures of the Composites Before
and After Extrusion
Figure 1 shows the microstructures of the composites with
different particle sizes before extrusion. It can be seen that the
SiC particles distribute more uniformly as the SiC particle size
increases. For the composites with small SiC particle size (4.7
and 16.7 lm), segregation of the SiC particles along the
surfaces of the Al powders is generally observed, similar to
many previous studies (Ref 16-19). As the SiC particle size
increases to 39.1 and 70.7 lm, a uniform distribution of the SiC
particles can be obtained.

Figure 3 shows the microstructures of the composites with

composites with small particle size (4.7 and 16.7 lm) has been

3.3 Hardness Evolution of the Composites
Before and After Extrusion
Figure 5 shows the HB hardness evolution of the com-
posites as a function of the particle size both before and after
extrusion. It can be seen that the hardness of the composites
before extrusion increases initially with the particle size up to
39.1 lm, after which the hardness starts to decrease. The
hardness evolution is similar to the density evolution of the
composites, as shown in Fig. 4. Since the pores will
substantially decrease the hardness, the composite reinforced
with 39.1- lm-sized SiC particles possesses highest hardness
before extrusion. It can also be seen that the hardness of the
composites after extrusion decreases with the increase of the
SiC particle size. It is widely accepted that for a given
particle volume fraction, smaller particle-reinforced composite
has higher hardness since it has more reinforcement/matrix
interfacial areas to transfer the externally applied load from
the matrix to the reinforcements (Ref 1, 6, 10, 20). Previous
study (Ref 21) also indicated that the larger SiC particles
make the matrix be more ductile but exhibit lower resistance
to the indentation deformation. Thus, the hardness of the
composites after extrusion decreases with the increase of the
SiC particle size. It should be noted that extrusion can
substantially improve the hardness of the composites, no
matter what particle size is, because of the increased
dislocation density and better interfacial bonding after
extrusion.

3.4 Tensile Properties of the Composites
Figure 6 illustrates the tensile mechanical properties of the
composite after extrusion. It can be seen that both the tensile
strength and yield strength of the composites decrease with
the increase of the particle size, while the elongation of the
composites increases with increasing the particle size after
extrusion. One can see that the tensile strength decrease
signicantly with the increase of the particle size, while the
yield strength decrease only slightly with the increase of the
particle size, indicating that particle size has signicant effect
on the work hardening behavior of the composites, instead of
the yield phenomenon. It is widely accepted that the
composites reinforced by smaller sized SiC particles have
larger reinforcement/matrix interfacial area and smaller inter-
particle spacing, which can transfer more load from the soft
matrix to the hard reinforcements, and thus to improve the
work hardening ability and strength of the composites
(Ref 10, 20).
3.5 Fracture Surfaces of the Composites
After Tensile Testing
Figure 7 shows the SEM fracture surfaces of the composites
after tensile testing. It can be seen that the fracture surfaces of
all the specimens have similar characteristics, including both
ductile and brittle fracture features. All the fracture surfaces
consist of numerous dimples in the matrix and fragmentation
and decohesion of the SiC particles from the matrix. The
dimples should be a result of the void nucleation and
subsequent coalescence by strong shear deformation and
fracture process on the shear plane, while the fracture and
decohesion of the SiC particles can be explained by work-
hardening and the fragmentation of the ceramic phase caused
by high stress concentration. The main difference of the
fracture surfaces is that the decohesion between the SiC
particles and Al matrix decreases, and the fragmentation of the
SiC particles increases as the SiC particle size increa

main difference of the
fracture surfaces is that the decohesion between the SiC
particles and Al matrix decreases, and the fragmentation of the
SiC particles increases as the SiC particle size increases. For the
composite reinforced with 4.7- lm-sized SiC particles, matrix

4. Discussion
4.1 Effect of Particle Size and Extrusion
on the Microstructures of the Composites
An important parameter of the metal matrix composites is
the distribution of the reinforcements. Among many factors
affecting the distribution of the SiC particles, the ratio between
the matrix powder size and reinforcement particle size (PRS) is
one of the most important factors. Stone and Tsakiropoulos
(Ref 22) found that the reinforcements will distribute more
uniformly when the PRS approaches unity. Bhanu Prasad et al.
(Ref 17) also used the geometrical method to explain the effect
of the relative PRS on the distributed uniformity of the
reinforcement particles. For the composites with large PRS, the
uniform distribution of SiC particles in the composites become
impossible because of the inadequate ratio of the surface areas
of matrix particles and SiC particles. In that case, the SiC
particles were found to be located along the Al powder surfaces
in the form of clusters to decrease the surface energy. In this
study, the SiC particles distribute more uniformly when the size
of the SiC particles is close to the size of the Al powders. The
density evolution in Fig. 4 is consistent with the microstructural
observations in Fig.

of the SiC particles is close to the size of the Al powders. The
density evolution in Fig. 4 is consistent with the microstructural
observations in Fig. 1, in which the composite reinforced with

It should be noted that extrusion can generate severe plastic
deformation of the matrix, which leads to the rearrangement of
the SiC particles, and thus, to the improvement in the
distributed uniformity of the SiC particles. In this study, one-
step extrusion is sufcient to eliminate the segregation of the

high stress generated during extrusion can also decrease the
number of pores and enhance the interfacial bonding between
the matrix and SiC particles, as shown in Fig. 3.

Properties of the Composites
Before extrusion, the hardness values of the composites with
the particle sizes of 4.7 and 70.7 lm are extremely low,
because of the voids and poor interfacial bonding of t

Before extrusion, the hardness values of the composites with
the particle sizes of 4.7 and 70.7 lm are extremely low,
because of the voids and poor interfacial bonding of the
composites. During deformation, the clustered SiC particles and
voids can be treated as pre-existing cracks, and thus the
externally applied stress cannot be transferred from the soft
matrix to the hard reinforcements, resulting in the degradation
of the mechanical properties, as was also shown in the previous

It is generally accepted that reducing the reinforcement size
can obtain a ner microstructure and improved mechanical

fraction, because of the smaller inter-particle spacing and larger
work hardening rate. The decrease in the particle size increases
both the effects of direct strengthening and indirect strength-
ening (Ref 23-25). As the SiC particle size decrease, the
interfacial area between the matrix and the SiC particles also
increase, and more load can thus be transferred from the matrix
to the SiC particles. It should be noted that a large interfacial
area can also facilitate the generation of more dislocations in
the matrix, thereby improving the mechanical properties of the
composites. On the other hand, the larger-sized particles
fracture more easily than the smaller ones during extrusion
and tensile testing process because of two reasons (Ref 26).
First, each larger-sized particle has larger interface area with the
matrix, and thus endures higher stress concentration.

uring extrusion
and tensile testing process because of two reasons (Ref 26).
First, each larger-sized particle has larger interface area with the
matrix, and thus endures higher stress concentration. Second,
the particle fracture strength is controlled by the intrinsic aws
within the particle. Since the size of a aw is limited by the size
of the particle, larger particles are more likely to fracture
because they have a greater statistical probability of containing
a aw that is greater than the critical size (Ref 27). Since the
fractured particles cannot withstand any load, but act as
preferential failure sites, the composites with larger SiC particle
size show degradation of mechanical properties.
It can be seen from Fig. 6 that the elongation of the
composites increases with the particle size, in agreement with
some previous studies (Ref 16, 28). This may be attributed to
the inter-particle spacing, which decreases with the decrease of
the SiC particle size for a given particle volume fraction. Song
and Xiao (Ref 28) indicated that the ductility (elongation) is
essentially determined by the inter-particle spacing. During
deformation, the geometrically necessary dislocations are
stored in the matrix, and a large interparticle spacing can
accommodate more dislocations during deformation between
two neighboring particles to improve the ductility of the
composites. However, some other researchers (Ref 1, 6, 23)
showed an opposite result, in which the ductility increases with
the decrease of the particle size, which might be due to the pre-
existing aws over the critical size in the large-sized SiC
particles. In reality, the relationship between ductility (elonga-
tion) and particle size may be controlled by a lot of factors, and
a further investigation is still required in the future.
5. Conclusion
In this article, SiC particle-reinforced pure Al composites
were fabricated by powder metallurgy method. The effects of
particle size and extrusion on the microstructures and mechan-
ical properties of the composites have

particle-reinforced pure Al composites
were fabricated by powder metallurgy method. The effects of
particle size and extrusion on the microstructures and mechan-
ical properties of the composites have been studied. It has been
shown that both particle size and extrusion have important
effects on the microstructures and mechanical properties of the
composites. The voids coexisting with the clustered and large-
sized SiC particles signicantly decrease the density and
mechanical properties of the composites. Extrusion can redis-
tribute the SiC particles in the matrix and decrease the number
of pores, thus make the SiC particles distribute more uniformly
in the matrix, and substantially enhance the interfacial bonding
strength. The decrease of the SiC particle size will improve the
tensile strength and yield strength because of the larger
interfacial surface area and larger work hardening rate, but
decrease the ductility of the composites because of the smaller
inter-particle spacing.

An Experimental Investigation
on Mechanical and Wear
Properties of Al7075/SiCp
Composites: Effect of SiC
Content and Particle Size
One of the major advantages of metal matrix composites (MMCs) is that their tailorable
properties meet the specic requirements of a particular application. This paper deals with
the experimental investigations done on the effects of the reinforcement particulate size
and content on the Al7075/SiC composite. The composites were manufactured using stir
casting technique. The effect of SiC particle size (25, 50, and 75 lm) and particulate con-
tent (5, 10, and 15 wt %) on the microstructural, mechanical properties, and wear rate of
the composites was studied and the results were analyzed for varied conditions of rein-
forcement. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) examinations were used to assess the dis-
persion of SiC particles reinforced into the matrix alloy and was found with reasonably
uniform with minimal particle agglomerations and with good interfacial bonding between
the particles and matrix material. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis conrmed the presence
of Al and SiC with the composite. The results of mechanical tests showed that the increas-
ing SiC particle size and content considerably enhanced the ultimate tensile strength and
hardness of the composites while the ductility at this condition was decreased. The highest
ultimate tensile strength of 310 MPa and hardness of 126 BHN were observed for the com-
posites containing 15 wt %. SiC at 75 lm. Lesser the wear resistance of the reference alloy
while it was enhanced up to 40 % with the composites. The wear resistance was increased
up to 1200 m of sliding distance for all the composites, whereas for the composite contain-
ing 75 lm SiC particles, it was extended up to 1800 m.

1 Introduction
Metal matrix composites (MMCs) have proven their potential-
ity for numerous specialized applications in aerospace and auto-
motive industries due to their high strength-to-weight ratio, good
fatigue and creep resistance, a high coefcient of thermal expan-
sion, excellent wear resistance, and good machinability. MMCs
are engineered by a systematic combination of two or more mate-
rials in which the tailored properties are achieved with the use of
existing facilities, equipment, and technology [ 1]. Especially,
there has been a lot of research done for the past decade, to
develop the diversied MMCs exploring the different combina-
tions of reinforcements and matrix alloys for their compatibility to
get the required properties. All together, the investigations were
reported as the overall properties of the MMCs are primarily
affected by the properties of matrix phase and reinforcement
phase. Next to them are the factors such as processing method,
process control variables, size, and shape of the reinforcement [ 2].
The processing methods commonly used to develop the MMC
composites are squeeze casting [ 3], powder metallurgy [ 4], and
liquid state casting [ 5]. Among these fabrication methods, to
produce bulk MMCs, stir casting, being a liquid state processing
technique, has been widely pronounced as it is efcient, cost-
effective, simple, and exible [ 6,7]. The present work involves
the development of Al/SiC MMCs through liquid stir casting
technique.

2 Literature Survey
Over the past decades, research on the development of alumi-
num matrix composites has been extensively done applying to a
wide range of structural and nonstructural components in different
manufacturing industries. These investigations were carried out
with the dispersion of suitable reinforcement into every possible
matrix under suitable processing routes in view of understanding
and predicting their behavior. It was reported that stir casting is
the liquid state processing suggested as a promising route for the
successful fabrication of bulk MMCs commercially available for
large production of components owing to its exibility and eco-
nomical among the available fabrication routes [ 8,9]. In stir cast-
ing, wettability, uniformity of dispersed reinforcement, and the
chemical reaction at the interface of matrix-reinforcement inter-
face are some of the control factors acknowledged as signicant
for producing the sound composite materials. The studies in the
literature [10,11] suggest that the wettability of the reinforced SiC
particles in the aluminum matrix could be improved by alloying
Mg and Si while the uniform dispersion could be achieved by the
selection of suitable stirring parameters [ 12]. Also, the chemical
reaction at the interfaces can be prevented by oxidation/coating of
SiC or alloying of Al matrix with Mg or Si [ 13]. Since the rein-
forcement plays a signicant role in withstanding against the
externally applied loads, the factors like particulate type, size, and
percentage content are also the prominent factors, which ascertain

3 Experimentation
Liquid state fabrication of metal matrix composites involves
the dispersion of reinforcement into the molten metal followed by
solidication. To get proper dispersion, chemical compatibility
along with the wettability and reactability of the reinforcement is
necessary. The wettability of SiC particulate in aluminum is poor
at lower temperatures, which results in weak interfacial bonding
[11]. While at high temperatures, the molten aluminum matrix
rapidly reacts with SiC particulate and forms brittle compounds,
which degrade corrosion resistance and mechanical properties of
the composite. A good matrix-reinforcement interface bonding is
possible only when matrix wets the entire surface of the reinforce-
ment. Therefore, the stir cast specimens in this investigation were
produced at controlled temperature and stirring speed. Figure 1
shows the schematic diagram of the experimental setup used for
manufacturing aluminum matrix composites.
Initially, the Al7075 alloy was kept in the graph

lled temperature and stirring speed. Figure 1
shows the schematic diagram of the experimental setup used for
manufacturing aluminum matrix composites.
Initially, the Al7075 alloy was kept in the graphite crucible and
heated to 800
/C14C, which is slightly above the liquidus temperature
of the alloy. The melt was then held at that temperature for 30 min
for degassing. The liquid was stirred at 250 rpm with a mild steel
impeller while injecting the SiC particles into the molten matrix.
Liquid stirring was continued for 5 min after the completion of
particle feeding. The SiC particles were preheated to 200
/C14C prior
to their inclusion in the molten alloy. After proper stirring, the liq-
uid composite was poured into a preheated steel cavity of size
160 /C2160 /C210 mm and was allowed to solidify under the ambient
conditions. Different composites were manufactured with the var-
ied SiC weight percentages of 5%, 10%, and 15% and particle
sizes of 25 lm, 50 lm, and 75 lm. During the process, argon gas
was blown continuously into the crucible to protect against the
atmosphere. To ascertain the size of the particles considered, the
scanning electron microscope (SEM) micrographs of the as-
received SiC particles are taken as shown in Figs. 2(a)2(c).
Figure 3 shows the photograph of the steel cavity and the solidi-
ed composite.

5 Conclusions
In this investigation, the experimental results on effects of parti-
cle size and particulate content on the mechanical and wear prop-
erties of SiC particulate reinforced Al7075 aluminum matrix
composites were reported. The following ndings can be
summarized:
(1) According to the SEM examination of the composites, the dis-
tribution of SiC particles reinforced into the matrix alloy was
relatively uniform with minimal particle agglomerations.
(2) The agglomerations and segregation of the reinforced SiC
particles were increased for the increased SiC particulate
content while the dispersion of SiC was decreased when
the SiC particle size was larger.
(3) A good interfacial bonding between the particles and
matrix alloy and the presence of Al and SiC in the compos-
ite was conrmed through XRD analysis.
(4) The increasing SiC particle size and content enhanced the
ultimate tensile strength of the composites signicantly. The
highest UTS of 310 MPa was observed with the composite
containing 15 wt %. SiC with 75 lm. However, the ductility
of the composite at this condition was decreased severely.
(5) The increased hardness was attributed to the increased SiC
particle size and wt %. The higher hardness of 126BHN

of extruded Al matrix nanocomposites

abstract
In this work, the effect of SiC particle size and its amount on both physical and mechanical properties of
Al matrix composite were investigated. SiC of particle size 70 nm, 10 lm and 40 lm, and Al powder of
particle size 60 lm were used. Composites of Al with 5 and 10 wt.% SiC were fabricated by powder met-
allurgy technique followed by hot extrusion. Phase composition and microstructure were characterized.
Relative density, thermal conductivity, hardness and compression strength were studied. The results
showed that the X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis indicated that the dominant components were Al and
SiC. Densiﬁcation and thermal conductivity of the composites decreased with increasing the amount of
SiC and increased with increasing SiC particle size. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) studies showed
that the distribution of the reinforced particle was uniform. Increasing the amount of SiC leads to higher
hardness and consequently improves the compressive strength of Al–SiC composite. Moreover, as the SiC
particle size decreases, hardness and compressive strength increase. The use of ﬁne SiC particles has a
similar effect on both hardness and compressive strength.

1. Introduction
Today, there is an increasing need worldwide for the advanced
materials in order to obtain the desired properties. This is because
a single material generally cannot meet the requirements of harsh
engineering environments. That is why the need for composites
with unique properties is growing every day. There is an increasing
trend towards using composite materials in order to achieve better
performance in engineering materials. Thus, production and appli-
cation of metal matrix composites (MMCs) have increased in re-
cent years [1].
During the last two decades, a lot of research has been con-
ducted on aluminum matrix composites (AMCs). An optimum
combination of high strength and ductility gives aluminum based
metal matrix composites (AMCs) a wide range of possible ad-
vanced applications. The most important AMCs applications in
aerospace and infrastructure industries are enabled by functional
properties including high structural efﬁciency, excellent wear
resistance, and attractive thermal and electrical characteristics
[2–5] .
Low wear resistance of pure aluminum is a serious drawback in
using it in many applications. Addition of ceramic particles to alu-
minum matrix would improve the strength, hardness, and both the
wear and corrosion resistance of the matrix [6,7]. Particle rein-
forcements are more favorable than ﬁber type, due to the better
control of microstructure and mechanical properties, by varying
the size and the volume fraction of the reinforcement [8]. SiC par-
ticles have been mostly selected as the reinforcement phase [9,10],
this is due to, the high wear resistance, superior high temperature
mechanical properties and low cost. Moreover, SiC does not react
with the matrix at high temperatures and does not create unde-
sired phases [11].

the high wear resistance, superior high temperature
mechanical properties and low cost. Moreover, SiC does not react
with the matrix at high temperatures and does not create unde-
sired phases [11]. Challenging technical issues are overcome
including compatibility between reinforcement and matrix, afford-
able primary and secondary processing techniques capable of ade-
quately controlling reinforcement distribution, engineering design
methodologies, and characterization and control of interfacial
properties [12].
Powder metallurgy is considered as a good technique in produc-
ing metal–matrix composites. An important advantage of this
method is its low processing temperature compared to melting
techniques. Therefore, interaction between the matrix and the
reinforcement phases is prevented. On the other hand, good distri-
bution of the reinforcing particles can be achieved [13]. Another
advantage of powder metallurgy technique is in its ability to man-
ufacture near net shape product with low cost [14]. Extrusion can
be applied from the beginning of the production or as a supple-
mentary step after compaction.
It is commonly recognized that both the particle amount and
particle size have obvious effects on the mechanical properties
of the composites. The voids coexisted with the clustered SiC
particles and around the large sized SiC particles can be treated

This work aims at studying the effect of particle size and

The prepared samples were investigated by XRD and the micro-

The idea of performing compression test was taken from the appli-

extent to which a material can be deformed in special metalwork-

2. Experimental work
2.1. Composite preparation
Aluminum powder with an average particle size of 60 lm and
99.9% purity was used as the matrix material ( Fig. 1a). SiC powder
with an average particle size of 70 nm was supplied from Alfa Ae-
sar. Fig. 1 b shows a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) pic-
ture of the particles. Micrometric SiC particles with two sizes of
10 lm and 40 lm were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Scanning elec-
tron microscopy (SEM) model a JEOL JSM- 5410, revealed the angu-
lar shape of the micrometric ceramic particles ( Fig. 1 c and d). The
powders were mixed using a planetary ball mill for an hour, with
the speed of 150 rpm. The ball to powder ratio was 10:1 and liquid
ethanol was used as a process control agent. Specimens were pre-
pared through powder metallurgy technique at a compaction pres-
sure of 500 MPa, then sintered at 570 /C176C in a vacuum furnace with
a heating rate of 10 /C176

cess control agent. Specimens were pre-
pared through powder metallurgy technique at a compaction pres-
sure of 500 MPa, then sintered at 570 /C176C in a vacuum furnace with
a heating rate of 10 /C176C per minute. The specimens were socked at
420 /C176C for 1 h, 470 /C176C for another hour and ﬁnally at 570 /C176C for
5 h. After sintering, the specimens were hot extruded at 550 /C176C
with an extrusion ratio of 9:1.
2.2. Composite characterization
The extruded samples were examined by X-ray diffraction
(XRD) using Cu Ka radiation with a wavelength of 1.5418 A over
a2 h range of 20–90 /C176. True densities of the composites were mea-
sured by using the Archimedes’ method (ASTM: B962-13) and
compared with theoretical densities to obtain varying degree of
densiﬁcation. The thermal conductivity can be calculated accord-
ing the Wiedemann–Franz Law. Previous publication by the
authors give a detailed description of the procedures [19].
Microstructure studies on the prepared composites were per-
formed with a JEOL JSM-5410 model scanning electron microscope
(SEM). In order to detect the Al, SiC and any oxide of Al and SiC par-
ticles, energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analyses were per-
formed. Hardness of both pure aluminum and composites were
determined using Vickers indentation technique with a load of
3 kg at room temperature. Compressive testing was conducted
using a universal testing machine, HU-F500KNI, according to stan-
dard ASTM: E9-09, at the room temperature and under a speed of
0.5 mm/min [20 or 21] . The compression test specimens were
cylindrical with a height of 9 mm and gauge diameter of 9 mm.
The specimen faces were polished with sandpaper up to grade
600 and then lubricated with graphite. The compression experi-
ments were terminated at 60% specimen strains.

Fig. 3 shows the effect of SiC particle size and amount on the

with increasing the SiC percent. It can be expected that in the
sintering process the SiC particles are very dense with random dis-
tribution due to its high melting point. The dense network formed
of SiC particles prevents to be dense [22]. In addition, the decline in
the pressing capacity of samples with increasing in the amount of
SiC is due to the higher hardness of SiC. Therefore, these compos-
ites have lower compressibility that results in lower densiﬁcation.

3.3. The effect of SiC particle size and its amount on the thermal
conductivity
Fig. 4 shows the thermal conductivity of Al and Al–SiC compos-
ites depends on the particle size and amount of SiC. Thermal
conductivity of composites decreases with increasing weight per-
centage of SiC. Thermal conductivity of a metal mainly depends
on the motion of electrons in the structure. Ceramic-based SiC
particles distort the structure and forms barrier for aluminum elec-
trons providing conductivity. Therefore, conductivity of compos-
ites decreases with increasing the amount of SiC [24–26] .
On the other hand, particle size of SiC also affects the thermal
conductivity of composite and it increase as particle size increases.
Thermal conductivity of composites increased with increasing par-
ticle size of SiC. Because with increasing particle size of SiC, added
into the pure aluminum, fewer electrons are scattered and thus
mobility and thermal conductivity increase [26]. This result is in
very good agreement with study of Hussain et al. [27].

Considering this ﬁgure also, it shows that decreasing the parti-
cle size will increase the composite hardness. Therefore, the aver-
age hardness of Al–SiC composite specimens with 70 nm SiC
particles is higher than that of the Al–SiC composite specimens
with 10 and 40 lm SiC particles. The reason for this increase can
be examined from two perspectives. One is due to greater interfa-
cial area between the hard and soft phases [35]. Secondly, the de-
fects in the coarse-grained particles are more than the ﬁne-grained
ones which results in its easy fracture under tension.
3.6. The effect of SiC particle size and its amount on the compressive
strength
Fig. 7 shows the stress–strain curves from compression tests of
aluminum composite reinforced with different SiC particle size and
amount after extrusion. A considerable difference is seen between
the strength of pure aluminum sample and the composite samples.
It is shown that the sample containing 10 wt.% SiC has generally
better strength than the 5 wt.% SiC and pure aluminum samples.
On the other hand, the observed changes in the strength may be
attributed to changes in the particle size of SiC. A close look at the
diagrams shows that the samples containing 70 nm SiC particles
have comparatively exceptional properties. Additionally, the
stress–strain behavior of the sample containing 40 lm particle
sizes is different from two other samples. The reason is due to
the great difference in their particle size.

Although tensile testing can determine if a proper bonding has
been produced, compression tests can also be helpful to study the
barreling and upsetting behavior of the compacts. Stronger bonds
among particles delay the crack initiation during deformation
especially at the time of barreling [36,37].
Fig. 8 illustrates the effect of the SiC particle size and amount on
the compressive strength at a strain of 0.6. The addition of up to
10 wt.% SiC to pure aluminum powder increases the compressive
strength. The reason is that SiC particles prevent the movement
of dislocations in pure aluminum matrix through dispersion
strengthening mechanism [29]. Additionally, according to Eq. (1),
increasing the amount of SiC leads to a decrease in the distance be-
tween the SiC particles. Decreasing the distance between the SiC
particles according to Eq. (3) will increase the required tension
for dislocations movement between the SiC particles leading to
an increase in the material strength.

4. Conclusions
The effects of particle size and amount of reinforcement compo-
nent on the some properties of extruded Al–SiC nanocomposites
were investigated. The following ﬁndings can be summarized:
1. The presence of Al and SiC were veriﬁed by XRD analysis
technique.
2. As the amount of SiC particles increase, the composite’s densi-
ﬁcation declines. Also, as the SiC particle size increases in the
composite samples, densiﬁcation increases.
3. Increasing the amount and reducing the size of SiC lead to
reduced thermal conductivity.
4. According to SEM micrographs, as the SiC particles size decreases,
the grain size and the distributi on of reinforcement decreases.
Furthermore, as the SiC amount decreases in the composite, grain
size and SiC particle distribution homogeneity increases.
5. Increasing the amount and reducing the size of SiC promote
high hardness in the composite.

5. Increasing the amount and reducing the size of SiC promote
high hardness in the composite. The highest hardness of 72
HV is attributed to the sample containing 10 wt.% of SiC with
70 nm particle size.
6. The ﬁner particle size of SiC presents greater compressive
strength. By increasing the amount of SiC, compressive strength
increases. The highest strength was 601 MPa, for the composite
containing 10 wt.% SiC with 70 nm.
